_metadata.description: BaselinePilot check definitions — MS Security Baseline + Operational Health checks
_metadata.severityScale: Critical = must fix, High = should fix, Medium = recommended, Low = consider
_metadata.weightScale: 1 = informational, 2 = recommended, 3 = important (default), 4 = strongly recommended, 5 = critical
_metadata.effortScale: Quick Win = config/toggle, Some Effort = planning/testing/downtime, Major Effort = infra redesign

checks.name: Disable SMBv1 Protocol
checks.description: Disable SMBv1 client driver and server to eliminate EternalBlue-class vulnerabilities
checks.impact: SMBv1 enabled exposes the system to known RCE exploits including EternalBlue/WannaCry.
checks.remediation: Set ConfigureSMBV1ClientDriver to 'Disable driver' and LanmanServer\Parameters\SMB1 to 0
checks.rationale: SMBv1 is a 30-year-old protocol with no encryption, no integrity checking, and known RCE vulnerabilities (EternalBlue/WannaCry). Microsoft has disabled SMBv1 by default since Windows 10 1709 and actively recommends removal. The SecGuide.admx 'ConfigureSMBV1ClientDriver' setting was introduced specifically for this baseline.

checks.name: Enable SmartScreen for Windows
checks.description: Enable Windows SmartScreen to warn or block unrecognized apps and files from the internet
checks.impact: Without SmartScreen, users can execute malicious downloads without any reputation-based warning.
checks.remediation: Set EnableSmartScreen to 1 and ShellSmartScreenLevel to 'Warn' or 'Block' via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: SmartScreen checks file reputation before execution. It blocks known-bad executables and shows warnings for unrecognized files downloaded from the internet. requires this enabled. Without SmartScreen, users can execute zero-day malware without any warning. The baseline requires ShellSmartScreenLevel='Block' or 'Warn'.

checks.name: Disable Lock Screen Camera
checks.description: Prevent enabling the camera on the lock screen to reduce physical access information leakage
checks.impact: Lock screen camera can expose user presence information and may be abused for social engineering.
checks.remediation: Set NoLockScreenCamera to 1 via GPO (Personalization) or Intune CSP
checks.rationale: Disabling the lock screen camera prevents an attacker with physical access from using the camera to capture images of the environment or users. Low severity but part of physical security hardening.

checks.name: Disable Lock Screen Slideshow
checks.description: Prevent lock screen slideshow to avoid displaying potentially sensitive images from user photo libraries
checks.impact: Slideshow on lock screen can expose personal or corporate images to passersby.
checks.remediation: Set NoLockScreenSlideshow to 1 via GPO (Personalization) or Intune CSP
checks.rationale: Lock screen slideshows may display user photos that reveal sensitive information (whiteboards, documents, organizational charts) to anyone who can see the screen.

checks.name: Enforce Machine Inactivity Lock Timeout
checks.description: Configure machine inactivity limit to lock the session after 15 minutes (900 seconds) of idle time
checks.impact: Without an inactivity timeout, unattended sessions remain unlocked indefinitely, allowing physical walk-up access.
checks.remediation: Set 'Interactive logon: Machine inactivity limit' to 900 via GPO or Intune CSP
checks.rationale: Without an inactivity timeout, an unattended workstation remains unlocked indefinitely. The 900-second (15-minute) baseline balances security with usability. requires session lock after a defined period.

checks.name: Enable PowerShell Script Block Logging
checks.description: Enable script block logging to record all PowerShell code executed on the system for forensic analysis
checks.impact: Without script block logging, malicious PowerShell usage leaves no trace, preventing incident investigation.
checks.remediation: Set EnableScriptBlockLogging to 1 via GPO (PowerShell\ScriptBlockLogging) or Intune
checks.rationale: Script Block Logging was added in PowerShell 5.0 specifically for security forensics. Without it, attackers using PowerShell-based tools (Mimikatz, Empire, Cobalt Strike) leave no trace. Microsoft made this a baseline requirement starting with Windows 10 1607 baseline.

checks.name: Restrict WinRM Client Basic Authentication
checks.description: Disable Basic authentication on the WinRM client to prevent cleartext credential transmission
checks.impact: Basic authentication sends credentials in cleartext, allowing network sniffing to capture admin passwords.
checks.remediation: Set AllowBasic to 0 under WinRM\Client via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: WinRM Basic authentication sends credentials in cleartext (Base64 encoded, trivially decoded). An attacker on the network can capture admin credentials with a simple packet sniffer. Must be disabled on both client and service.

checks.name: Restrict WinRM Unencrypted Traffic
checks.description: Disable unencrypted WinRM traffic on both client and service to enforce transport security
checks.impact: Unencrypted WinRM traffic exposes administrative commands and data to network interception.
checks.remediation: Set AllowUnencryptedTraffic to 0 for both WinRM\Client and WinRM\Service via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: Unencrypted WinRM traffic exposes administrative commands and data to network interception. Even with NTLM/Kerberos auth, the command payloads are visible.

checks.name: Disable AutoPlay for All Drive Types
checks.description: Disable autoplay/autorun for all drives to prevent automatic execution of media content
checks.impact: AutoPlay automatically executes code from removable media, enabling USB-based malware delivery (Stuxnet-class).
checks.remediation: Set NoDriveTypeAutoRun to 255 (all types) and NoAutorun to 1 via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: AutoPlay/AutoRun automatically executes content from inserted media. This is the mechanism used by Stuxnet and Conficker to spread via USB drives. NoDriveTypeAutoRun=255 disables AutoPlay for all drive types.

checks.name: Secure Remote Desktop Encryption Level
checks.description: Set RDP encryption to High to protect session data with strong encryption
checks.impact: Low RDP encryption allows session data to be intercepted and decoded by a network attacker.
checks.remediation: Set MinEncryptionLevel to 3 (High) via GPO (Terminal Services) or Intune CSP
checks.rationale: RDP encryption level must be set to High (3) to use maximum key strength (128-bit). Low or Client Compatible encryption uses weaker keys that can be broken by a network attacker.

checks.name: Require RDP Network Level Authentication
checks.description: Enable Network Level Authentication for RDP to authenticate before session creation
checks.impact: Without NLA, unauthenticated users reach the Windows login screen, exposing the RDP service to pre-auth attacks.
checks.remediation: Set UserAuthentication to 1 via GPO (Terminal Services\Security) or Intune CSP
checks.rationale: Network Level Authentication (NLA) requires the user to authenticate before the full RDP session is created. Without NLA, unauthenticated users reach the Windows login screen, exposing the service to BlueKeep-class pre-auth RCE vulnerabilities (CVE-2019-0708).

checks.impact: Cached domain credentials can be extracted offline to compromise the domain account.
checks.remediation: Set "Network access: Do not allow storage of passwords and credentials for network authentication" to Enabled
checks.description: Do not allow storage of passwords and credentials for network authentication to prevent credential caching
checks.name: Disable Domain Credential Storage
checks.rationale: Credential caching stores domain credentials locally, allowing offline logon. While convenient, cached credentials can be extracted and cracked offline. Setting DisableDomainCreds=1 prevents storage of credentials for network authentication.

checks.impact: Without SEHOP, attackers can use SEH overwrite exploits for code execution.
checks.remediation: Set DisableExceptionChainValidation to 0 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\kernel
checks.description: Enable SEHOP to protect against structured exception handler overwrite exploits
checks.name: Enable Structured Exception Handling Overwrite Protection
checks.rationale: Structured Exception Handling Overwrite Protection (SEHOP) prevents a class of stack-based buffer overflow exploits that overwrite the SEH chain. Introduced in Windows Vista SP1 as a mitigation against common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Without a legal banner, unauthorized users may claim they were unaware the system was restricted.
checks.remediation: Set LegalNoticeCaption and LegalNoticeText under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System
checks.description: Display a legal notice banner before user logon to establish legal accountability for system access
checks.name: Configure Legal Notice Before Logon
checks.rationale: A legal notice banner establishes accountability — users cannot claim they were unaware the system was restricted. Required by many compliance frameworks ( , PCI DSS 2.3). The baseline requires both caption and text to be configured.

checks.impact: Remote Assistance can be abused for unauthorized session monitoring or control.
checks.remediation: Set fAllowUnsolicited to 0 and fAllowToGetHelp to 0 under Terminal Services
checks.description: Prevent unsolicited Remote Assistance offers and configure solicited assistance timeout
checks.name: Disable Remote Assistance Connections
checks.rationale: Remote Assistance allows a remote user to view or control the desktop. Unsolicited offers can be social-engineered. recommends disabling both solicited and unsolicited assistance on sensitive systems.

checks.impact: NTLM authentication is vulnerable to relay and pass-the-hash attacks.
checks.remediation: Set RestrictSendingNTLMTraffic to 2 (Deny all) or 1 (Audit) under Lsa\MSV1_0
checks.description: Restrict NTLM authentication and audit NTLM usage to reduce pass-the-hash attack surface
checks.name: Restrict NTLM Authentication to Domain
checks.rationale: NTLM traffic restriction (RestrictSendingNTLMTraffic) controls whether the machine sends NTLMv1/v2 responses. Setting to 'Deny all' forces Kerberos-only authentication, eliminating NTLM relay and pass-the-hash attack vectors. Start with 'Audit' mode to identify impact.

checks.impact: WDigest stores plaintext passwords in LSASS memory, trivially extractable by Mimikatz.
checks.remediation: Set UseLogonCredential to 0 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest
checks.description: Prevent WDigest from storing plaintext credentials in memory (Mimikatz target)
checks.name: Disable WDigest Plaintext Credential Storage
checks.rationale: WDigest stores plaintext passwords in LSASS memory. This was the #1 credential theft vector exploited by Mimikatz. KB2871997 added the UseLogonCredential registry value to disable this. Baseline requirement since Windows 8.1/Server 2012 R2.

checks.impact: If the Event Log service is stopped, no security events are recorded.
checks.remediation: Set Windows Event Log service (EventLog) start type to Automatic
checks.description: Ensure the Windows Event Log service is running and set to Automatic start
checks.name: Enable Windows Event Log Service
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Without command-line auditing, process creation events show the executable but not what arguments were used.
checks.remediation: Set ProcessCreationIncludeCmdLine_Enabled to 1
checks.description: Include command line data in process creation audit events (4688)
checks.name: Enable Audit Process Creation Command Line
checks.rationale: Process creation command line auditing (Event 4688) records not just what process started but what arguments were passed. Without this, you see 'cmd.exe started' but not 'cmd.exe /c net user admin P@ssw0rd /add'. Essential for incident investigation.

checks.impact: Anonymous named pipe access allows unauthenticated users to enumerate shares and user accounts.
checks.remediation: Set restrictnullsessaccess to 1 under LanManServer\Parameters
checks.description: Restrict anonymous access to named pipes and shares to prevent information disclosure
checks.name: Disable Anonymous Access to Named Pipes
checks.rationale: Anonymous named pipe access allows unauthenticated users to enumerate shares, users, and domain information. This is routinely exploited during the reconnaissance phase of attacks.

checks.impact: ICS can create an uncontrolled bridge between corporate and external networks.
checks.remediation: Set NC_ShowSharedAccessUI to 0 via GPO or Intune
checks.description: Prevent users from enabling Internet Connection Sharing on network connections
checks.name: Disable Internet Connection Sharing
checks.rationale: Internet Connection Sharing (ICS) creates an uncontrolled bridge between the corporate network and another network (WiFi, cellular). This bypasses all network security controls.

checks.impact: Solicited Remote Assistance can be social-engineered to grant full desktop control.
checks.remediation: Set fAllowToGetHelp to 0 via GPO (Remote Assistance) or Intune
checks.description: Prevent users from requesting remote assistance to reduce attack surface
checks.name: Disable Solicited Remote Assistance
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Without secure RPC, RDP management traffic can be intercepted or tampered with.
checks.remediation: Set fEncryptRPCTraffic to 1 under Terminal Services
checks.description: Require secure RPC communication for Remote Desktop connections
checks.name: Require Secure RPC Communication for RDP
checks.rationale: Without secure RPC communication for RDP, the management traffic between client and server is unprotected. An attacker could intercept and modify RDP session setup parameters.

checks.impact: Disconnected RDP sessions with no timeout consume resources and remain exploitable.
checks.remediation: Set MaxDisconnectionTime and MaxIdleTime under Terminal Services
checks.description: Configure idle and disconnected session time limits for RDP
checks.name: Set RDP Session Disconnect Timeout
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Saved RDP passwords allow anyone with the .rdp file to connect without re-authentication.
checks.remediation: Set DisablePasswordSaving to 1 under Terminal Services\Client
checks.description: Prevent saving of passwords in Remote Desktop Connection (.rdp) files
checks.name: Disable Storing Passwords in RDP Files
checks.rationale: Saved RDP passwords in .rdp files allow anyone with file access to connect without re-authentication. In shared environments, this provides persistent unauthorized access.

checks.impact: Drive redirection allows full file system access between RDP client and host.
checks.remediation: Set fDisableCdm to 1 under Terminal Services
checks.description: Prevent client drive redirection in Remote Desktop sessions to block data exfiltration
checks.name: Disable RDP Drive Redirection
checks.rationale: RDP drive redirection maps the client's file system into the RDP session, allowing easy data exfiltration. Disabling clipboard and drive redirection is a data loss prevention control for sensitive environments.

checks.impact: Without prompt, cached RDP credentials allow automatic reconnection.
checks.remediation: Set fPromptForPassword to 1 under Terminal Services
checks.description: Require password entry for every RDP connection regardless of client settings
checks.name: Always Prompt for Password on RDP Connection
checks.rationale: Without always prompting for password on RDP connection, cached credentials allow automatic reconnection. This means a stolen .rdp file or saved connection provides permanent access without knowing the password.

checks.impact: AlwaysInstallElevated allows any user to install MSI packages with SYSTEM privileges.
checks.remediation: Set AlwaysInstallElevated to 0 under both HKLM and HKCU Installer policies
checks.description: Prevent Windows Installer from using elevated permissions for all installations
checks.name: Disable Windows Installer Always Install Elevated
checks.rationale: AlwaysInstallElevated is a well-known privilege escalation vector — it allows any MSI package to install with SYSTEM privileges. Penetration testers routinely check for this. Must be disabled on both HKLM and HKCU.

checks.impact: Print Spooler has been the target of many RCE vulnerabilities (PrintNightmare CVE-2021-34527).
checks.remediation: Set Spooler service start type to Disabled unless the machine requires local printing
checks.description: Disable the Print Spooler service to prevent PrintNightmare-class vulnerabilities
checks.name: Disable Print Spooler on Non-Print Servers
checks.rationale: The Print Spooler service has been the target of PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-34527, CVSS 8.8), which allows remote code execution as SYSTEM. Even after patches, new Spooler vulnerabilities continue to be discovered. Microsoft recommends disabling the service on non-print servers.

checks.impact: LLMNR can be poisoned by any host on the local network to capture NTLM hashes.
checks.remediation: Set EnableMulticast to 0 under DNSClient policy
checks.description: Disable LLMNR to prevent LLMNR poisoning attacks for credential theft
checks.name: Disable Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution
checks.rationale: LLMNR poisoning is a trivial network attack — any host on the local network can respond to LLMNR queries and capture NTLM hashes. Tools like Responder automate this. Disabling LLMNR forces DNS-only resolution.

checks.impact: NetBIOS name resolution can be poisoned on the local network for NTLM relay attacks.
checks.remediation: Set NetBIOS over TCP/IP to Disabled on all network adapters via DHCP option or manually
checks.description: Disable NetBIOS name resolution to prevent NBNS poisoning attacks
checks.name: Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP
checks.rationale: NetBIOS Name Service (NBNS) poisoning is similar to LLMNR — an attacker on the local network can respond to name queries and relay NTLM credentials. Setting NodeType=2 (P-node) disables broadcast-based name resolution.

checks.impact: Blank passwords combined with network logon give unauthenticated remote access.
checks.remediation: Set LimitBlankPasswordUse to 1 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa
checks.description: Prevent accounts with blank passwords from being used for network logon
checks.name: Restrict Use of Blank Passwords to Console Only
checks.rationale: If blank passwords are allowed for network logon, any local account without a password can be accessed remotely. LimitBlankPasswordUse=1 restricts blank passwords to console-only logon.

checks.impact: Autorun commands execute automatically when media is inserted without user consent.
checks.remediation: Set NoAutorun to 1 under Explorer policies
checks.description: Set default autorun behavior to not execute any autorun commands
checks.name: Disable Autorun for Default AutoRun Behavior
checks.rationale: The default autorun behavior executes autorun commands when media is inserted. Setting NoAutorun=1 prevents any autorun.inf commands from executing. Defense-in-depth alongside NoDriveTypeAutoRun.

checks.impact: SmartScreen blocks executables with no reputation or known-bad reputation.
checks.remediation: Set ShellSmartScreenLevel to Block under SystemSettings
checks.description: Configure SmartScreen for Windows Explorer to warn or block unrecognized executables
checks.name: Configure Windows Defender SmartScreen for Explorer
checks.rationale: SmartScreen for Explorer provides reputation-based blocking for executables downloaded from the internet. ShellSmartScreenLevel='Block' automatically blocks unrecognized files rather than just warning.

checks.impact: Without enhanced anti-spoofing, facial recognition can be bypassed with photos or masks.
checks.remediation: Set EnhancedAntiSpoofing to 1 under FacialFeatures biometrics policy
checks.description: Enable enhanced anti-spoofing for Windows Hello facial recognition
checks.name: Enable Enhanced Anti-Spoofing for Face Authentication
checks.rationale: Enhanced anti-spoofing for Windows Hello facial recognition uses IR cameras and depth sensors to distinguish real faces from photos, masks, or 3D models. Without this, facial recognition can be bypassed with a high-resolution photo.

checks.impact: Microsoft accounts bypass domain authentication policies and GPO controls.
checks.remediation: Set NoConnectedUser to 3 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System
checks.description: Prevent users from adding or signing in with Microsoft accounts on domain-joined devices
checks.name: Block Microsoft Accounts for Authentication
checks.rationale: Microsoft accounts on domain-joined devices bypass Group Policy controls and may sync data to personal OneDrive. NoConnectedUser=3 blocks adding or signing in with Microsoft accounts on enterprise devices.

checks.impact: Search indexer consuming excessive resources can delay security event processing.
checks.remediation: Configure Search indexer backoff via Group Policy or service priority
checks.description: Ensure Windows Search service does not degrade security event collection performance
checks.name: Disable Windows Search Indexer Backoff
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Remote Shell provides an alternate remote execution path outside normal RDP/WinRM controls.
checks.remediation: Set AllowRemoteShellAccess to 0 under WinRM\Service\WinRS
checks.description: Disable WinRS/Remote Shell service to reduce remote code execution attack surface
checks.name: Disable Windows Remote Shell Service
checks.rationale: Windows Remote Shell (WinRS) provides an alternate remote execution path outside WinRM. Disabling it reduces the remote code execution attack surface. Only relevant if WinRM is enabled.

checks.impact: Digest authentication sends a reversible hash of the password over the network.
checks.remediation: Set AllowDigest to 0 under WinRM\Client
checks.description: Disable Digest authentication on WinRM client to prevent weak credential transmission
checks.name: Configure Digest Authentication for WinRM Client
checks.rationale: Digest authentication sends a reversible hash of the password. While better than Basic (cleartext), Digest hashes can be captured and cracked offline. AllowDigest=0 forces Kerberos/NTLM only.

checks.impact: Hash publication can expose content verification data.
checks.remediation: Set HashPublicationForPeerCaching to 0 via Group Policy
checks.description: Disable BranchCache hash publication to prevent content verification bypass
checks.name: Disable Hash Publication for Distributed Caching
checks.rationale: BranchCache hash publication enables peer-to-peer content distribution. Disabling it on most endpoints reduces the attack surface for cache poisoning.

checks.impact: Rename the built-in Administrator account to deter targeted attacks
checks.description: Rename the built-in Administrator account to deter targeted attacks
checks.remediation: GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > System Access > Set 'NewAdministratorName'. Intune: Use Settings Catalog > System Access or Endpoint Security profile.
checks.name: Rename Built-in Administrator Account
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Rename the built-in Guest account
checks.description: Rename the built-in Guest account
checks.remediation: GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > System Access > Set 'NewGuestName'. Intune: Use Settings Catalog > System Access or Endpoint Security profile.
checks.name: Rename Built-in Guest Account
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.description: Ensure the Guest account is disabled
checks.name: Disable Guest Account
checks.impact: Ensure the Guest account is disabled
checks.remediation: GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > System Access > Set 'EnableGuestAccount' to 0. Intune: Use Settings Catalog > System Access or Endpoint Security profile.
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.description: Force Advanced Audit Policy to override category-level audit policy
checks.name: Force Audit Policy Subcategory Settings Override
checks.impact: Force Advanced Audit Policy to override category-level audit policy
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'SCENoApplyLegacyAuditPolicy' to 1 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa. Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\SCENoApplyLegacyAuditPolicy. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.rationale: When legacy audit policy and Advanced Audit Policy Configuration both exist, legacy wins and overrides the granular settings. SCENoApplyLegacyAuditPolicy=1 forces Advanced Audit Policy to take precedence, enabling per-subcategory control.

checks.description: Digitally sign communications (always) for SMB server
checks.name: Configure SMB Session Signing Requirement (Server)
checks.impact: Digitally sign communications (always) for SMB server
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'RequireSecuritySignature' to 1 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManServer\Parameters. Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManServer\Parameters\RequireSecuritySignature. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.rationale: SMB server signing (RequireSecuritySignature=True) ensures all SMB packets are cryptographically signed. Without server-side enforcement, clients can negotiate unsigned connections vulnerable to NTLM relay attacks.

checks.description: Digitally sign communications (always) for SMB client
checks.name: Configure SMB Session Signing Requirement (Client)
checks.impact: Digitally sign communications (always) for SMB client
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'RequireSecuritySignature' to 1 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanWorkstation\Parameters. Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanWorkstation\Parameters\RequireSecuritySignature. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.rationale: SMB client signing ensures the client also verifies packet integrity. Both client and server must require signing to prevent downgrade attacks where an attacker forces unsigned communication.

checks.description: Enable safe DLL search order to prevent DLL hijacking attacks
checks.name: Disable Safe DLL Search Mode Override
checks.impact: Enable safe DLL search order to prevent DLL hijacking attacks
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'SafeDllSearchMode' to 1 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager. Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\SafeDllSearchMode. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.rationale: Safe DLL Search Mode changes the DLL search order to search system directories before the current working directory. Without this, a malicious DLL in the current directory is loaded before the legitimate system DLL (DLL hijacking).

checks.impact: Warn on unsigned driver installation to prevent loading of malicious drivers
checks.description: Warn on unsigned driver installation to prevent loading of malicious drivers
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'Policy' under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Driver Signing. Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Driver Signing\Policy. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.name: Configure Unsigned Driver Installation Behavior
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.description: Set screen saver grace period to 0 so password protection is immediate
checks.name: Configure Screen Saver Grace Period
checks.impact: Set screen saver grace period to 0 so password protection is immediate
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'ScreenSaverGracePeriod' to 0 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon. Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\ScreenSaverGracePeriod. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.description: Prevent anonymous users from translating SIDs to account names
checks.name: Disable Anonymous SID/Name Translation
checks.impact: Prevent anonymous users from translating SIDs to account names
checks.remediation: GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > System Access > Set 'LSAAnonymousNameLookup' to 0. Intune: Use Settings Catalog > System Access or Endpoint Security profile.
checks.rationale: Anonymous SID/name translation allows unauthenticated users to determine admin account names from well-known SIDs (S-1-5-21-..-500). This aids targeted password attacks against the actual Administrator account.

checks.description: Strengthen default permissions of Windows system objects
checks.name: Configure Security Options System Objects Default Permissions
checks.impact: Strengthen default permissions of Windows system objects
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'ProtectionMode' to 1 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager. Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\ProtectionMode. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.rationale: ProtectionMode=1 strengthens the default DACL on system objects (named pipes, events, mutexes), preventing standard users from modifying objects created by SYSTEM.

checks.impact: Restrict remote access to the registry to authorized services only
checks.description: Restrict remote access to the registry to authorized services only
checks.remediation: PowerShell: Set-Service -Name 'RemoteRegistry' -StartupType <Automatic|Disabled>. GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > System Services. Intune: Use custom script or Settings Catalog.
checks.name: Disable Remote Access to Registry Paths
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Disable gaming services (XblAuthManager, XblGameSave, XboxNetApiSvc) on enterprise devices
checks.description: Disable gaming services (XblAuthManager, XblGameSave, XboxNetApiSvc) on enterprise devices
checks.remediation: PowerShell: Set-Service -Name 'XblAuthManager' -StartupType <Automatic|Disabled>. GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > System Services. Intune: Use custom script or Settings Catalog.
checks.name: Disable Xbox Services on Enterprise Devices
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Configure firewall to not merge local rules with GPO rules on domain profile
checks.description: Configure firewall to not merge local rules with GPO rules on domain profile
checks.remediation: GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Domain Profile > Set 'AllowLocalFirewallRules'. Intune: Endpoint Security > Firewall profile.
checks.name: Configure Windows Defender Firewall Merge Rules
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.description: Disable consumer experiences, tips, and suggestions on enterprise devices
checks.name: Disable Consumer Experience Features
checks.impact: Disable consumer experiences, tips, and suggestions on enterprise devices
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'DisableWindowsConsumerFeatures' to 1 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent. Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent\DisableWindowsConsumerFeatures. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.rationale: Consumer experiences include app suggestions, tips, and Start menu advertisements. These features contact external servers and may install unwanted apps. DisableWindowsConsumerFeatures=1 removes these from enterprise devices.

checks.impact: Recommend installing apps from Microsoft Store for verified sources
checks.description: Recommend installing apps from Microsoft Store for verified sources
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'ConfigureAppInstallControl' under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\SmartScreen. Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\SmartScreen\ConfigureAppInstallControl. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.name: Prevent App Installations from Store Only (Recommended)
checks.rationale: Part of the Microsoft Security Baseline hardening recommendations. These settings reduce the attack surface by disabling unnecessary features, enforcing secure defaults, and preventing common exploitation techniques.

checks.impact: Verify CPU vulnerability mitigations (Spectre V2, Meltdown) are enabled
checks.description: Verify CPU vulnerability mitigations (Spectre V2, Meltdown) are enabled
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'FeatureSettingsOverride' under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management. Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management\FeatureSettingsOverride. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.name: Enable Spectre/Meltdown Mitigations
checks.rationale: Spectre V2 and Meltdown CPU vulnerabilities allow kernel memory disclosure from user-mode processes. FeatureSettingsOverride controls the OS-level mitigations. The baseline ensures mitigations are active.

checks.description: Configure Print Spooler to not accept client connections remotely

checks.name: Enable Real-Time Protection in Defender
checks.description: Ensure Microsoft Defender real-time protection is active and not disabled by policy
checks.impact: Without real-time protection, malware executes undetected. This is the most fundamental endpoint security control.
checks.remediation: Set DisableRealtimeMonitoring to 0 (not configured). Verify no third-party AV is disabling Defender.
checks.rationale: Real-time protection is the most fundamental endpoint security control. Without it, malware executes undetected between signature-based scans. The Intune baseline requires this since the very first MDM baseline.

checks.name: Enable Behavior Monitoring in Defender
checks.description: Enable behavior monitoring to detect malware based on runtime behavior patterns, not just signatures
checks.impact: Behavior monitoring catches zero-day and fileless malware that signature-based scanning misses.
checks.remediation: Set DisableBehaviorMonitoring to 0 via GPO (Defender\Real-Time Protection) or Intune
checks.rationale: Behavior monitoring detects malware based on runtime actions (file drops, registry changes, process injection) rather than signatures. This catches zero-day and fileless malware that signature-only scanning misses.

checks.name: Enable Cloud-Delivered Protection
checks.description: Enable cloud protection with extended timeout for real-time threat intelligence from Microsoft's cloud
checks.impact: Cloud protection provides seconds-fast detection of new threats using Microsoft's global threat intelligence.
checks.remediation: Set MAPSReporting to 2 (Advanced) and CloudBlockLevel to 2 (High+) via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: Cloud-delivered protection sends suspicious file metadata to Microsoft's cloud for real-time analysis. Detection response time drops from hours (signature update) to seconds. MAPSReporting=2 enables Advanced membership.

checks.name: Enable PUA Protection in Defender
checks.description: Enable Potentially Unwanted Application protection to block adware, bundleware, and toolbars
checks.impact: PUA silently installs alongside legitimate software, degrading performance and potentially exfiltrating data.
checks.remediation: Set PUAProtection to 1 (Enabled) via GPO (Defender) or Intune
checks.rationale: Potentially Unwanted Applications (PUA) include adware, bundleware, and toolbars that degrade performance and may exfiltrate data. PUAProtection=1 blocks these at download time. The baseline has required this since Windows 10 1809.

checks.name: Keep Defender Signatures Current
checks.description: Ensure antivirus signature definitions are no more than 2 days old
checks.impact: Stale signatures miss detection of recently discovered malware. Signature age >2 days indicates update failure.
checks.remediation: Verify Windows Update delivery of signature updates. Check MpCmdRun.exe -SignatureUpdate. Verify proxy/firewall allows access to *.wdcp.microsoft.com.
checks.rationale: Antivirus signatures are the primary detection mechanism for known malware. Signature age >2 days means newly discovered threats go undetected. Microsoft releases signature updates multiple times daily. Verify *.wdcp.microsoft.com is accessible.

checks.name: Configure and Enforce Credential Guard
checks.description: Enable VBS with Credential Guard to protect NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets from theft
checks.impact: Without Credential Guard, credential hashes in LSASS can be extracted by tools like Mimikatz.
checks.remediation: 1. Enable 'Turn On Virtualization Based Security' via GPO (System > Device Guard) or Intune CSP (DeviceGuard/EnableVirtualizationBasedSecurity). 2. Set Platform Security Level to 'Secure Boot' (not 'Secure Boot and DMA Protection' unless you can confirm DMA support on all devices). 3. Enable Credential Guard set to 'Enabled without lock' initially, then switch to 'Enabled with UEFI lock' after validation. 4. Do NOT enable 'Require UEFI Memory Attributes Table' unless tested — incompatible firmware can cause BSOD or data loss. 5. Enable Kernel Mode Hardware Enforced Stack Protection in enforcement mode (22H2+).
checks.rationale: Credential Guard uses Virtualization Based Security (VBS) to isolate NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets. Platform Security Level should be set to 'Secure Boot' (not 'Secure Boot and DMA Protection') because if hardware doesn't support DMA protection, selecting the stronger option prevents Credential Guard from operating entirely. Microsoft changed this recommendation after discovering many deployments where CG failed to start. The 'Require UEFI Memory Attributes Table' option should NOT be set unless confirmed compatible — devices without UEFI MAT support may experience crashes or data loss. Start without UEFI lock to allow easy rollback, then enforce UEFI lock after validation. System Guard Secure Launch requires a supported processor. Kernel Mode Hardware Enforced Stack Protection (new in 22H2) provides kernel code protection — enabled if hardware supports it, no-op otherwise.

checks.name: Enable LSA Protection (RunAsPPL)
checks.description: Enable LSA protection to prevent unauthorized code from running in the LSASS process
checks.impact: Without LSA protection, credential-dumping tools can inject into LSASS and extract all cached credentials.
checks.remediation: Windows 11 22H2+: Configure via GPO 'System > Local Security Authority > Configure LSASS to run as a protected process' — set to 'Enabled without UEFI Lock' initially. Once validated, switch to 'Enabled with UEFI Lock'. Older OS: Use SecGuide.admx 'LSA Protection' setting. Intune CSP: Device/Vendor/MSFT/Policy/Config/LocalSecurityAuthority/ConfigureLsaProtectedProcess. TEST FIRST: review https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/security/credentials-protection-and-management/configuring-additional-lsa-protection for known issues.
checks.rationale: LSA protection (RunAsPPL) prevents unauthorized code injection into LSASS. First introduced in Windows 8.1 as part of Pass-the-Hash mitigations. In Windows 11 22H2+, a new inbox setting 'Configure LSASS to run as a protected process' (System > Local Security Authority) replaces the older SecGuide.admx 'LSA Protection' setting. The new setting adds an 'Enabled without UEFI Lock' option (RunAsPPL=2) for easier rollback. The 24H2 MDM baseline specifies 'Enabled with UEFI lock' (CSP value 1, registry RunAsPPL=1). Microsoft recommends starting WITHOUT UEFI lock to allow rollback if LSA protection breaks any LSA plugins or drivers, then enforcing UEFI lock once validated. Previous OS versions must continue using the SecGuide.admx setting. Review Microsoft documentation for potential issues — some third-party credential providers may be incompatible.

checks.impact: Malicious macros spawn child processes (cmd, powershell, wscript) to execute payloads.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule D4F940AB-401B-4EFC-AADC-AD5F3C50688A to Block (1) or Audit (2)
checks.description: Attack Surface Reduction rule to block Office applications from creating child processes
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block Office Macros from Creating Child Processes
checks.rationale: ASR rule D4F940AB: Office macros frequently spawn child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, wscript.exe) to execute payloads. This rule blocks Office apps from creating child processes, stopping the most common malware delivery chain.

checks.impact: Malicious documents drop executables for persistence and payload delivery.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule 3B576869-A4EC-4529-8536-B80A7769E899 to Block (1)
checks.description: Prevent Office applications from writing executable files to disk
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block Office Apps from Creating Executable Content
checks.rationale: ASR rule 3B576869: Malicious documents drop executable files to disk for persistence. This rule prevents Office apps from writing executable content (.exe, .dll, .scr) to any location.

checks.impact: JS/VBS engines are used to download and execute malware payloads.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule D3E037E1-3EB8-44C8-A917-57927947596D to Block (1)
checks.description: Block JavaScript and VBScript engines from executing downloaded content
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block JavaScript and VBScript from Launching Downloaded Content
checks.rationale: ASR rule D3E037E1: JavaScript and VBScript engines (wscript.exe, cscript.exe) are abused to download and execute malware. This rule blocks scripts from launching downloaded content.

checks.impact: Obfuscated scripts are a primary technique for evading antivirus detection.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule 5BEB7EFE-FD9A-4556-801D-275E5FFC04CC to Block (1)
checks.description: Block obfuscated scripts that attempt to evade static analysis
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block Execution of Potentially Obfuscated Scripts
checks.rationale: ASR rule 5BEB7EFE: Script obfuscation techniques (encoding, string concatenation, variable substitution) are used to bypass static analysis. This rule uses machine learning to detect and block obfuscated scripts.

checks.impact: Win32 API calls from macros enable process injection and memory manipulation.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule 92E97FA1-2EDF-4476-BDD6-9DD0B4DDDC7B to Block (1)
checks.description: Block Office macros from making Win32 API calls which could inject code
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block Win32 API Calls from Office Macros
checks.rationale: ASR rule 92E97FA1: Win32 API calls from Office macros enable process injection, memory manipulation, and privilege escalation. This rule blocks macros from using API calls that are commonly abused.

checks.impact: LSASS credential dumping is the primary technique for lateral movement.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule 9E6C4E1F-7D60-472F-BA1A-A39EF669E4B2 to Block (1)
checks.description: Block credential stealing from the Windows Local Security Authority Subsystem
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block Credential Stealing from LSASS
checks.rationale: ASR rule 9E6C4E1F blocks credential stealing from LSASS by monitoring for suspicious access patterns (the same patterns used by Mimikatz, ProcDump, and Task Manager credential dump). This is a defense-in-depth layer alongside Credential Guard.

checks.impact: PSExec and WMI are primary tools for lateral movement in enterprise attacks.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule D1E49AAC-8F56-4280-B9BA-993A6D77406C to Block (1)
checks.description: Block process creations originating from PSExec and WMI commands
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block Process Creation from PSExec and WMI
checks.rationale: ASR rule D1E49AAC: PSExec and WMI are the most common tools for lateral movement in enterprise attacks. This rule blocks process creation from PSExec/WMI commands, limiting attacker mobility.

checks.impact: USB-delivered executables bypass network-level security controls.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule B2B3F03D-6A65-4F7B-A9C7-1C7EF74A9BA4 to Block (1)
checks.description: Block untrusted and unsigned executable processes that run from USB removable drives
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block Untrusted and Unsigned Processes from USB
checks.rationale: ASR rule B2B3F03D: USB-delivered executables bypass network security controls entirely. This rule blocks untrusted/unsigned processes from USB and removable drives.

checks.impact: Ransomware protection uses cloud heuristics to identify ransomware behavior patterns.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule C1DB55AB-C21A-4637-BB3F-A12568109D35 to Block (1)
checks.description: Enable advanced ransomware protection using cloud intelligence
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Use Advanced Protection Against Ransomware
checks.rationale: ASR rule C1DB55AB: Advanced ransomware protection uses cloud-based machine learning to identify ransomware behavior patterns (mass file encryption, shadow copy deletion). Provides real-time response to new ransomware variants.

checks.impact: Email is the primary delivery vector for malware executables.
checks.remediation: Set ASR rule BE9BA2D9-53EA-4CDC-84E5-9B1EEEE46550 to Block (1)
checks.description: Block executable content from email client and webmail
checks.name: Enable ASR Rule: Block Email Client Executable Content
checks.rationale: ASR rule BE9BA2D9: Email is the primary malware delivery vector. This rule blocks executable content (EXE, DLL, SCR, scripts) from email clients and webmail, preventing direct execution of malicious attachments.

checks.impact: Network protection blocks C2 callbacks and malicious web content at the network layer.
checks.remediation: Set EnableNetworkProtection to 1 (Enabled) via GPO or Intune
checks.description: Enable network protection to block connections to malicious domains and IPs
checks.name: Enable Network Protection in Defender
checks.rationale: Network Protection extends SmartScreen to all outbound HTTP/HTTPS traffic, blocking connections to known-malicious domains and IPs. This catches C2 (command and control) callbacks and drive-by downloads that SmartScreen alone misses.

checks.impact: Controlled Folder Access prevents unauthorized applications from modifying files in protected folders.
checks.remediation: Set EnableControlledFolderAccess to 1 via Intune. Test with Audit mode (2) first.
checks.description: Enable Controlled Folder Access for ransomware protection of user data folders
checks.name: Enable Controlled Folder Access
checks.rationale: Controlled Folder Access prevents unauthorized applications from modifying files in protected folders (Documents, Desktop, etc.). This is a direct defense against ransomware encryption. Start with Audit mode (2) before Block mode (1).

checks.impact: Without scheduled scans, dormant threats on disk go undetected between real-time checks.
checks.remediation: Set ScheduleDay and ScheduleTime for quick and full scans via GPO or Intune
checks.description: Ensure scheduled quick scans run daily and full scans run weekly
checks.name: Configure Defender Scan Schedule
checks.rationale: Without scheduled scans, dormant malware files on disk go undetected between real-time protection checks. The baseline requires daily quick scans (ScheduleDay=0) and configurable full scans.

checks.impact: Sample submission enables rapid response to new malware variants.
checks.remediation: Set SubmitSamplesConsent to 1 (Send safe samples) via GPO or Intune
checks.description: Allow automatic sample submission to help Microsoft identify new threats
checks.name: Enable Defender Sample Submission
checks.rationale: Sample submission enables Microsoft to analyze suspicious files and create targeted signatures within hours. SubmitSamplesConsent=1 sends only safe samples (no documents) automatically. Essential for zero-day response.

checks.impact: System-level exploit mitigations prevent common memory corruption attacks.
checks.remediation: Configure Exploit Protection settings via Intune or ProcessMitigationOptions policy
checks.description: Verify Exploit Protection system-level mitigations (DEP, ASLR, CFG) via Get-ProcessMitigation -System. REG_BINARY MitigationOptions requires manual review.
checks.name: Enable Exploit Protection System Settings
checks.rationale: Exploit Guard system-level mitigations include DEP (Data Execution Prevention), ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization), CFG (Control Flow Guard), and SEHOP. These make memory corruption exploits significantly harder.

checks.impact: Without tamper protection, malware can disable Defender before executing its payload.
checks.remediation: Enable Tamper Protection via Microsoft 365 Defender portal or Intune
checks.description: Prevent unauthorized changes to Defender security settings
checks.name: Enable Tamper Protection for Defender
checks.rationale: Tamper Protection prevents malware from disabling Defender before executing its payload. Without it, a simple registry edit or service stop command disables all endpoint protection. Requires Intune or MDE portal to manage.

checks.impact: LOLBins (certutil, mshta, regsvr32, rundll32) are abused for malware download and execution.

checks.impact: Without download scanning, malware can be saved to disk undetected.

checks.rationale: On-Access protection (IOAV) scans files as they are downloaded or created. DisableIOAVProtection=0 ensures every file is checked before it can be opened. Without this, malware can be saved to disk and executed later.

checks.name: Enforce Minimum Password Length of 14 Characters
checks.description: Set minimum password length to at least 14 characters per Microsoft security baseline
checks.impact: Short passwords are exponentially easier to brute-force. 14 characters is the baseline minimum.
checks.remediation: Set MinimumPasswordLength to 14 via GPO (Account Policy) or Intune CSP (DeviceLock/MinDevicePasswordLength)
checks.rationale: The 14-character minimum was introduced in the Windows 10 21H2 baseline (previously 8). The 22H2 baseline blog specifically discussed this change.

checks.name: Enforce Password Complexity Requirements
checks.description: Require passwords to contain uppercase, lowercase, digits, and special characters
checks.impact: Simple passwords are trivially cracked with dictionary attacks even at longer lengths.
checks.remediation: Set PasswordComplexity to 1 (Enabled) via GPO (Account Policy) or Intune
checks.rationale: Password complexity requires at least 3 of 4 character types (uppercase, lowercase, digits, special). Without complexity, users choose simple passwords ('password123') that are trivially cracked with dictionary attacks.

checks.name: Configure Account Lockout Threshold
checks.description: Set account lockout to trigger after 10 or fewer failed logon attempts
checks.impact: Without lockout, attackers can brute-force passwords indefinitely with no throttling.
checks.remediation: Set LockoutBadCount to 10 via GPO (Account Lockout Policy) or Intune
checks.rationale: Account lockout threshold of 10 was set in the 22H2 baseline (previously 0/not configured). This balances security (blocks brute-force) with usability (10 attempts before lockout is generous for legitimate users).

checks.name: Enforce LAN Manager Authentication Level
checks.description: Set LAN Manager authentication to 'Send NTLMv2 response only. Refuse LM & NTLM' to prevent legacy hash exposure
checks.impact: LM and NTLMv1 hashes are trivially crackable. Allowing them exposes all credentials to rainbow table attacks.
checks.remediation: Set LmCompatibilityLevel to 5 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: LmCompatibilityLevel=5 forces NTLMv2 only and refuses LM and NTLMv1 responses. LM hashes are trivially crackable (rainbow tables), and NTLMv1 is vulnerable to relay attacks. This has been a baseline requirement since Windows Server 2008.

checks.name: Prevent Storage of LAN Manager Hash
checks.description: Prevent Windows from storing the weak LAN Manager hash of passwords on next password change
checks.impact: Stored LM hashes can be extracted and cracked in seconds, revealing the cleartext password.
checks.remediation: Set NoLMHash to 1 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: The LAN Manager hash is a 14-character DES-based hash that can be cracked in seconds using rainbow tables. NoLMHash=1 prevents Windows from storing this weak hash on next password change.

checks.name: Restrict Anonymous SAM Enumeration
checks.description: Prevent anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts to hide user account names from unauthenticated callers
checks.impact: Anonymous SAM enumeration lets attackers harvest valid usernames for targeted password attacks.
checks.remediation: Set RestrictAnonymousSAM to 1 and RestrictAnonymous to 1 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa
checks.rationale: Anonymous SAM enumeration allows unauthenticated callers to list all user accounts and groups via the NetBIOS null session. Attackers use this for username harvesting before password spraying. RestrictAnonymousSAM=1 blocks this.

checks.impact: Passwords that never expire provide indefinite access if compromised.
checks.remediation: Set MaximumPasswordAge to 60 or less via Account Policy
checks.description: Set maximum password age to 60 days or less per security baseline
checks.name: Enforce Maximum Password Age
checks.rationale: Microsoft DROPPED password expiration from the security baseline in v1903 (May 2019), calling it 'an ancient and obsolete mitigation of very low value.' NIST 800-63B also recommends against routine rotation when using strong passwords and MFA. This check is retained as ORGANIZATIONAL policy for environments without passwordless/MFA. Organizations with MFA should consider removing this requirement.

checks.impact: Without a minimum age, users can cycle through passwords to reuse old ones.
checks.remediation: Set MinimumPasswordAge to 1 via Account Policy
checks.description: Set minimum password age to at least 1 day to prevent rapid password cycling
checks.name: Enforce Minimum Password Age
checks.rationale: Without a minimum password age, users can cycle through passwords immediately to return to their favorite. MinimumPasswordAge=1 prevents this by requiring at least 1 day between changes. Works with password history.

checks.impact: Without password history, users can reuse compromised passwords indefinitely.
checks.remediation: Set PasswordHistorySize to 24 via Account Policy
checks.description: Remember at least 24 previous passwords to prevent password reuse
checks.name: Enforce Password History of 24 Passwords
checks.rationale: Password history (24 remembered) prevents users from cycling back to previously used passwords. Combined with minimum age, this forces genuinely new passwords each rotation.

checks.impact: Short lockout durations allow rapid brute-force retry cycles.
checks.remediation: Set LockoutDuration to 15 or more via Account Lockout Policy
checks.description: Set account lockout duration to at least 15 minutes
checks.name: Configure Account Lockout Duration
checks.rationale: A 15-minute lockout duration balances security (blocks brute-force) with usability (legitimate users don't have to wait long).

checks.impact: Short reset times allow spreading brute-force attempts over brief intervals.
checks.remediation: Set ResetLockoutCount to 15 or more via Account Lockout Policy
checks.description: Set lockout counter reset time to at least 15 minutes
checks.name: Reset Account Lockout Counter After 15 Minutes
checks.rationale: The lockout counter reset time determines how long between failed attempts before the counter resets. 15 minutes prevents spreading brute-force attempts over brief intervals.

checks.impact: Reversible encryption stores passwords in a way that can be decrypted to plaintext.
checks.remediation: Set ClearTextPassword to 0 via Account Policy
checks.description: Ensure passwords are not stored using reversible encryption
checks.name: Enable Reversible Password Encryption Disabled
checks.rationale: Reversible encryption stores passwords in a form that can be decrypted back to cleartext. This is required only for CHAP authentication (rare). The baseline requires ClearTextPassword=0 to prevent this critical exposure.

checks.impact: User-initiated elevation bypasses application control policies.
checks.remediation: Set EnableInstallerDetection to 1 under Policies\System
checks.description: Prevent standard users from installing user-level applications with elevated privileges
checks.name: Restrict Privilege Escalation from Standard Users
checks.rationale: Authentication and credential security is critical for preventing unauthorized access. Weak password policies and credential storage mechanisms are among the most exploited vulnerabilities in enterprise environments.

checks.impact: Auto admin logon stores cleartext password in the registry and bypasses all authentication.
checks.remediation: Set AutoAdminLogon to 0 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon
checks.description: Prevent automatic administrative logon to the console
checks.name: Disable Auto Admin Logon
checks.rationale: Auto admin logon stores the administrator password in cleartext in the registry at HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\DefaultPassword. Any user or malware with registry read access gets the admin password.

checks.impact: Without SAS, a fake logon screen can capture user credentials.
checks.remediation: Set DisableCAD to 0 under Winlogon policies
checks.description: Enable the requirement for Ctrl+Alt+Del before logon to prevent credential capture
checks.name: Require Ctrl+Alt+Del for Logon
checks.rationale: The Secure Attention Sequence (Ctrl+Alt+Del) cannot be intercepted by software, ensuring the logon prompt is genuine Windows. Without this requirement, a fake logon screen could capture credentials.

checks.impact: Cached credentials can be extracted and cracked offline for domain compromise.
checks.remediation: Set CachedLogonsCount to 4 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon
checks.description: Limit cached logon credentials to 4 or fewer to reduce offline attack surface
checks.name: Configure Cached Logon Credentials Limit
checks.rationale: Cached logon credentials allow domain users to log on when the DC is unreachable. CachedLogonsCount=4 limits the number of cached credentials, reducing the attack surface for offline credential extraction.

checks.name: Investigate Excessive Failed Logon Attempts
checks.description: Detect patterns of failed logon events (4625) indicating brute-force attacks or misconfigured service accounts
checks.impact: Sustained failed logon attempts may indicate credential stuffing, password spraying, or misconfigured apps.
checks.remediation: Review top source IPs and usernames. For service accounts: verify stored credentials. For external IPs: consider blocking.
checks.rationale: Sustained failed logon patterns (>100 in 30 days) indicate credential stuffing, password spraying, or misconfigured service accounts. The ODA approach: identify top source IPs and usernames, then investigate — service accounts with wrong cached passwords are the #1 cause.

checks.name: Resolve Recurring Account Lockouts
checks.description: Identify accounts with repeated lockouts (4740) indicating credential attacks or stale cached credentials
checks.impact: Repeated lockouts cause helpdesk burden and often indicate cached credentials using an old password.
checks.remediation: Check CallerComputerName in event 4740 to identify the source machine. Clear Credential Manager, check scheduled tasks.
checks.rationale: Recurring lockouts cause helpdesk burden and indicate stale cached credentials. Check CallerComputerName in Event 4740 to find the source machine, then clear Credential Manager and check scheduled tasks, mapped drives, and cached RDP connections.

checks.impact: Off-hours admin logons may indicate unauthorized access or compromised credentials.
checks.remediation: Review admin logon events between 22:00-06:00 and on weekends. Verify with the administrators.
checks.description: Investigate administrator logon events (4624) occurring outside business hours
checks.name: Review Off-Hours Administrative Logons
checks.rationale: Administrator logons between 22:00-06:00 or on weekends may indicate compromised credentials being used when legitimate admins are away. Correlate with known maintenance windows and on-call schedules.

checks.impact: Kerberos pre-auth failures from multiple users suggest a coordinated password spray.

checks.impact: Unauthorized admin group membership grants full system control.
checks.remediation: Review 4728/4732 events. Verify all admin group additions were authorized.
checks.description: Review events for accounts added to the local Administrators group (4728/4732)
checks.name: Investigate New Administrator Group Members
checks.rationale: Unauthorized additions to the local Administrators group (Events 4728/4732) grant full system control. This is a critical persistence mechanism — attackers add their own accounts after initial compromise.

checks.impact: Confirm LAPS or Windows LAPS is deployed for local admin password management
checks.remediation: Legacy LAPS: Set AdmPwdEnabled=1 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft Services\AdmPwd. Windows LAPS (preferred): Configure via Intune LAPS policy or GPO 'Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > LAPS'. Intune CSP: ./Device/Vendor/MSFT/LAPS/Policies/BackupDirectory.
checks.name: Verify Local Admin Password Solution (LAPS) Deployment
checks.description: Confirm LAPS or Windows LAPS is deployed for local admin password management
checks.rationale: LAPS ensures each machine has a unique, random, regularly-rotated local admin password. Legacy LAPS (MSI-based) used the AdmPwd registry key. Windows LAPS (built-in since 21H2) is now the recommended solution and uses different CSP/registry paths. The 24H2 MDM baseline configures 'LAPS > Backup Directory: Backup to Azure AD only'. This check validates legacy LAPS deployment — consider also verifying Windows LAPS via the LAPS CSP (BackupDirectory setting).

checks.name: Enable Credential Validation Auditing
checks.description: Configure 'Audit Credential Validation' to record Success and Failure events
checks.impact: Without credential validation auditing, failed authentication attempts leave no trace.
checks.remediation: Set 'Audit Credential Validation' to 'Success and Failure' via GPO (Advanced Audit Policy) or Intune
checks.rationale: Credential Validation (subcategory of Account Logon) records NTLM/local authentication attempts. Events 4774/4776 show successful and failed credential checks. Without this, password spraying against local accounts is invisible.

checks.name: Enable Security Group Management Auditing
checks.description: Audit all changes to security groups including additions, removals, and modifications
checks.impact: Unaudited group changes allow privilege escalation to go undetected.
checks.remediation: Set 'Audit Security Group Management' to 'Success and Failure' via Advanced Audit Policy
checks.rationale: Security Group Management auditing records when users are added to or removed from security groups (Events 4728-4735). Unaudited group changes allow privilege escalation to persist undetected.

checks.name: Enable Logon/Logoff Auditing
checks.description: Audit successful and failed logon and logoff events for security monitoring
checks.impact: Without logon auditing, unauthorized access cannot be detected or investigated.
checks.remediation: Set 'Audit Logon' and 'Audit Logoff' to 'Success and Failure' via Advanced Audit Policy
checks.rationale: Logon auditing (Events 4624/4625) is the foundation of security monitoring. Without it, you cannot detect unauthorized access, track user activity, or investigate incidents.

checks.name: Enable Process Creation Auditing
checks.description: Enable process creation auditing to log all new process starts including command-line arguments
checks.impact: Without process auditing, malware execution and LOLBin abuse leaves no audit trail.
checks.remediation: Set 'Audit Process Creation' to 'Success' via Advanced Audit Policy. Also enable command-line logging.
checks.rationale: Process Creation auditing (Event 4688) records every process start. Combined with command-line logging (SEC-019), this provides complete visibility into what executes on the system. Essential for malware detection and incident response.

checks.name: Enable Sensitive Privilege Use Auditing
checks.description: Audit use of sensitive privileges (SeDebugPrivilege, SeTakeOwnership, etc.) for privilege escalation detection
checks.impact: Sensitive privilege abuse is the primary mechanism for post-exploitation lateral movement.
checks.remediation: Set 'Audit Sensitive Privilege Use' to 'Success and Failure' via Advanced Audit Policy
checks.rationale: Sensitive Privilege Use auditing (Events 4672/4673) records when users exercise powerful privileges like SeDebugPrivilege, SeTakeOwnership, or SeBackupPrivilege. These are the privileges attackers need for lateral movement and persistence.

checks.impact: Without account logon auditing, local authentication events leave no trace.
checks.remediation: Set "Audit Account Logon Events" to "Success and Failure"
checks.description: Audit successful and failed account logon events for NTLM/local authentication
checks.name: Enable Account Logon Auditing
checks.rationale: Audit policies determine what security events Windows records. Without proper auditing, security incidents cannot be detected, investigated, or proven. The baseline ensures comprehensive logging for forensic readiness.

checks.impact: Unaudited account changes allow unauthorized account creation to persist.
checks.remediation: Set "Audit User Account Management" to "Success and Failure"
checks.description: Audit user account creation, deletion, and modification events
checks.name: Enable User Account Management Auditing
checks.rationale: Audit policies determine what security events Windows records. Without proper auditing, security incidents cannot be detected, investigated, or proven. The baseline ensures comprehensive logging for forensic readiness.

checks.impact: Process creation auditing is essential for malware execution detection.
checks.remediation: Set "Audit Process Creation" to "Success"
checks.description: Audit process creation events for endpoint detection
checks.name: Enable Detailed Tracking - Process Creation
checks.rationale: Audit policies determine what security events Windows records. Without proper auditing, security incidents cannot be detected, investigated, or proven. The baseline ensures comprehensive logging for forensic readiness.

checks.impact: Without file auditing, unauthorized data access goes undetected.
checks.remediation: Set "Audit File System" to "Failure" or "Success and Failure"
checks.description: Enable audit of file system access for sensitive resource monitoring
checks.name: Enable Object Access - File System Auditing
checks.rationale: Audit policies determine what security events Windows records. Without proper auditing, security incidents cannot be detected, investigated, or proven. The baseline ensures comprehensive logging for forensic readiness.

checks.impact: Unaudited policy changes allow attackers to weaken security silently.
checks.remediation: Set "Audit Policy Change" to "Success and Failure"
checks.description: Audit changes to security policies including audit policy changes
checks.name: Enable Policy Change Auditing
checks.rationale: Audit Policy Change auditing (Events 4719/4907) detects when someone modifies the audit configuration itself. An attacker's first action after gaining access is often to disable auditing. This creates a tamper-evident trail.

checks.impact: Authentication policy changes can downgrade security without detection.
checks.remediation: Set "Audit Authentication Policy Change" to "Success"
checks.description: Audit changes to authentication policies (Kerberos, NTLM settings)
checks.name: Enable Authentication Policy Change Auditing

checks.description: Confirm that configured audit policies are producing actual events — zero events indicates broken auditing
checks.impact: A configured policy with zero events means you think you're logging but you're not — a silent failure.
checks.remediation: For each configured audit subcategory with zero events: verify log is not full, force gpupdate, generate test event.
checks.rationale: A configured audit policy with zero matching events means the audit subsystem has silently failed. This can happen when the Security log is full, the audit service crashed, or Group Policy hasn't propagated. This is a critical operational gap.

checks.name: Ensure Adequate Security Event Log Size
checks.description: Security event log should be at least 192 MB (196608 KB) per the 24H2 baseline to prevent rapid event overwrite
checks.impact: A small Security log overwrites events within hours, destroying forensic evidence.
checks.remediation: Set Security log maximum size to at least 196608 KB (192 MB) via GPO or Intune. Consider SIEM forwarding for long-term retention.
checks.rationale: The 24H2 MDM baseline increased the Security event log from 20 MB to 192 MB (196608 KB). The default 20 MB can fill in hours on busy systems, overwriting forensic evidence. With SIEM forwarding, this large local buffer ensures events survive network delays.
