checks.name: Enforce SMB Signing on Client and Server
checks.description: Require SMB packet signing to prevent man-in-the-middle tampering of file share traffic
checks.impact: Without SMB signing, an attacker on the network can intercept and modify SMB traffic (relay attacks).
checks.remediation: Set RequireSecuritySignature to True for both LanmanServer and LanManWorkstation via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: SMB signing prevents man-in-the-middle attacks on file share traffic. Without signing, an attacker can intercept and modify SMB packets (NTLM relay). The 25H2 baseline requires RequireSecuritySignature=True for both client and server.

checks.name: Enable Windows Firewall for All Profiles
checks.description: Ensure Windows Defender Firewall is enabled for Domain, Private, and Public profiles
checks.impact: A disabled firewall allows unrestricted inbound connections, exposing all listening services.
checks.remediation: Enable Windows Defender Firewall for all profiles via GPO or Intune. Set DefaultInboundAction to Block.
checks.rationale: A disabled Windows Firewall allows unrestricted inbound connections, exposing all listening services (RDP, SMB, WinRM, etc.) to the network. All three profiles (Domain, Private, Public) must be enabled.

checks.name: Disable Insecure TLS Protocols
checks.description: Disable SSL 2.0, SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1 — only TLS 1.2+ should be enabled
checks.impact: Legacy TLS/SSL protocols have known cryptographic weaknesses exploitable by POODLE, BEAST, and similar attacks.
checks.remediation: Set Enabled=0 and DisabledByDefault=1 for SSL 2.0/3.0 and TLS 1.0/1.1 under SCHANNEL\Protocols
checks.rationale: SSL 2.0/3.0 have POODLE (CVE-2014-3566), BEAST, and DROWN vulnerabilities. TLS 1.0/1.1 were deprecated by Microsoft in September 2023 and by IETF in RFC 8996. Only TLS 1.2+ provides adequate protection.

checks.name: Enforce Secure Channel Signing and Sealing
checks.description: Require signing and encryption of secure channel (Netlogon) traffic for domain member communication
checks.impact: Unsigned Netlogon channels are vulnerable to Zerologon (CVE-2020-1472) and NTLM relay attacks.
checks.remediation: Set RequireSignOrSeal=1, SealSecureChannel=1, SignSecureChannel=1 under Netlogon\Parameters
checks.rationale: Unsigned Netlogon channels are vulnerable to Zerologon (CVE-2020-1472, CVSS 10.0), the most critical AD vulnerability in recent history. RequireSignOrSeal=1 with SealSecureChannel=1 prevents MITM attacks on domain communication.

checks.impact: Without firewall logging, blocked connection attempts are invisible.
checks.remediation: Enable firewall logging with LogDroppedPackets=True for Domain profile
checks.description: Enable firewall logging for dropped packets on the Domain profile
checks.name: Enable Windows Firewall Logging for Domain Profile
checks.rationale: Network security settings control protocol behavior, encryption requirements, and firewall rules. These settings protect data in transit and prevent network-based attacks like MITM, relay, and poisoning.

checks.impact: Private profile firewall drops should be logged for investigation.
checks.remediation: Enable firewall logging with LogDroppedPackets=True for Private profile
checks.description: Enable firewall logging for dropped packets on the Private profile
checks.name: Enable Windows Firewall Logging for Private Profile
checks.rationale: Network security settings control protocol behavior, encryption requirements, and firewall rules. These settings protect data in transit and prevent network-based attacks like MITM, relay, and poisoning.

checks.impact: Public profile is the most exposed and most critical to log.
checks.remediation: Enable firewall logging with LogDroppedPackets=True for Public profile
checks.description: Enable firewall logging for dropped packets on the Public profile
checks.name: Enable Windows Firewall Logging for Public Profile
checks.rationale: Network security settings control protocol behavior, encryption requirements, and firewall rules. These settings protect data in transit and prevent network-based attacks like MITM, relay, and poisoning.

checks.impact: Allow-by-default inbound rules expose all services to the network.
checks.remediation: Set DefaultInboundAction to Block for Domain, Private, and Public profiles
checks.description: Configure all firewall profiles to block inbound by default
checks.name: Set Default Inbound Action to Block for All Profiles
checks.rationale: DefaultInboundAction=Block ensures that only explicitly allowed inbound traffic reaches the system. Without this, the firewall operates in allow-by-default mode, negating its purpose.

checks.impact: Firewall notifications on public networks prompt users to allow connections.
checks.remediation: Set NotifyOnListen to False for Public profile
checks.description: Suppress firewall notifications on the Public profile to prevent social engineering
checks.name: Disable Firewall Notifications for Public Profile
checks.rationale: Network security settings control protocol behavior, encryption requirements, and firewall rules. These settings protect data in transit and prevent network-based attacks like MITM, relay, and poisoning.

checks.impact: mDNS enables network-based name resolution poisoning attacks.
checks.remediation: Set EnableMDNS to 0 under DNSClient policy
checks.description: Disable multicast DNS to prevent network discovery and name poisoning
checks.name: Disable Multicast DNS (mDNS)
checks.rationale: Network security settings control protocol behavior, encryption requirements, and firewall rules. These settings protect data in transit and prevent network-based attacks like MITM, relay, and poisoning.

checks.impact: Unsigned LDAP traffic can be intercepted and modified (LDAP relay).
checks.remediation: Set LDAPClientIntegrity to 1 (Negotiate signing) under Lsa
checks.description: Require LDAP client to perform signing for integrity protection
checks.name: Require LDAP Client Signing
checks.rationale: LDAP signing prevents MITM attacks on LDAP traffic. Without signing (LDAPClientIntegrity=1), an attacker can intercept and modify LDAP queries and responses, leading to authentication bypass.

checks.impact: Source routing allows attackers to specify the route packets take through the network.
checks.remediation: Set DisableIPSourceRouting to 2 under TCPIP6\Parameters
checks.description: Disable IPv6 source routing to prevent routing manipulation attacks
checks.name: Configure IPv6 Source Routing Protection
checks.rationale: IPv6 source routing allows an attacker to specify the route packets take, bypassing firewalls and IDS. DisableIPSourceRouting=2 (highest protection) drops all source-routed packets.

checks.impact: Source routing bypasses network security devices by specifying packet paths.
checks.remediation: Set DisableIPSourceRouting to 2 under Tcpip\Parameters
checks.description: Disable IPv4 source routing to prevent routing manipulation attacks
checks.name: Configure IPv4 Source Routing Protection
checks.rationale: IPv4 source routing has the same risks as IPv6 — attackers specify the packet route to bypass security controls. DisableIPSourceRouting=2 drops all source-routed packets.

checks.impact: ICMP redirects can be used to reroute traffic through an attacker-controlled host.
checks.remediation: Set EnableICMPRedirect to 0 under Tcpip\Parameters
checks.description: Prevent ICMP redirects from overriding OSPF-generated routes
checks.name: Enable ICMP Redirect Prevention
checks.rationale: ICMP redirects can reroute traffic through an attacker-controlled host, enabling MITM attacks. EnableICMPRedirect=0 prevents ICMP redirect messages from overriding routing table entries.

checks.impact: ISATAP creates an uncontrolled IPv6 tunnel that bypasses IPv4 security controls.
checks.remediation: Disable ISATAP interface via netsh interface isatap set state disabled
checks.description: Disable ISATAP to prevent IPv6 tunneling over IPv4 networks
checks.name: Disable IP Source Routing for ISATAP
checks.rationale: Network security settings control protocol behavior, encryption requirements, and firewall rules. These settings protect data in transit and prevent network-based attacks like MITM, relay, and poisoning.

checks.impact: Without SMB encryption, file contents are visible to network sniffers.
checks.remediation: Set EncryptData to True via Set-SmbServerConfiguration
checks.description: Require SMB 3.0 encryption for all connections
checks.name: Require SMB Encryption
checks.rationale: SMB encryption (EncryptData=True) protects file share data in transit. Without encryption, file contents are visible to network sniffers. Required for compliance with data protection regulations when sensitive data is accessed via file shares.

checks.impact: Completely disable SSL 2.0 protocol
checks.remediation: Registry: HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\SecurityProviders\\SCHANNEL\\Protocols\\<Protocol>\\<Client|Server> — Set 'Enabled' to 0 and 'DisabledByDefault' to 1 for legacy protocols. GPO: Use IISCrypto tool or custom ADMX. Intune: Custom OMA-URI.
checks.name: Disable SSL 2.0 Server and Client
checks.description: Completely disable SSL 2.0 protocol
checks.rationale: SSL 2.0 (1995) has no security — completely broken cryptography. Must be explicitly disabled via SCHANNEL registry because some legacy applications may still attempt to negotiate it.

checks.impact: Completely disable SSL 3.0 protocol (POODLE vulnerability)
checks.remediation: Registry: HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\SecurityProviders\\SCHANNEL\\Protocols\\<Protocol>\\<Client|Server> — Set 'Enabled' to 0 and 'DisabledByDefault' to 1 for legacy protocols. GPO: Use IISCrypto tool or custom ADMX. Intune: Custom OMA-URI.
checks.name: Disable SSL 3.0 Server and Client
checks.description: Completely disable SSL 3.0 protocol (POODLE vulnerability)
checks.rationale: SSL 3.0 (1996) is vulnerable to POODLE (CVE-2014-3566) which allows plaintext recovery from encrypted connections. Deprecated by IETF in RFC 7568. Must be disabled on both client and server.

checks.impact: Disable TLS 1.0 protocol (deprecated)
checks.remediation: Registry: HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\SecurityProviders\\SCHANNEL\\Protocols\\<Protocol>\\<Client|Server> — Set 'Enabled' to 0 and 'DisabledByDefault' to 1 for legacy protocols. GPO: Use IISCrypto tool or custom ADMX. Intune: Custom OMA-URI.
checks.name: Disable TLS 1.0 Server and Client
checks.description: Disable TLS 1.0 protocol (deprecated)
checks.rationale: TLS 1.0 (1999) is deprecated by Microsoft (Sept 2023) and IETF (RFC 8996). Vulnerable to BEAST and CRIME attacks. Still widely used by legacy applications — test before disabling.

checks.impact: Disable TLS 1.1 protocol (deprecated)
checks.remediation: Registry: HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\SecurityProviders\\SCHANNEL\\Protocols\\<Protocol>\\<Client|Server> — Set 'Enabled' to 0 and 'DisabledByDefault' to 1 for legacy protocols. GPO: Use IISCrypto tool or custom ADMX. Intune: Custom OMA-URI.
checks.name: Disable TLS 1.1 Server and Client
checks.description: Disable TLS 1.1 protocol (deprecated)
checks.rationale: TLS 1.1 (2006) is deprecated alongside TLS 1.0. While not as vulnerable, it lacks modern cipher suites and AEAD encryption. No reason to keep enabled when TLS 1.2+ is universally supported.

checks.name: Enable BitLocker Drive Encryption
checks.description: Ensure BitLocker is enabled on the OS volume with XTS-AES-256 encryption
checks.impact: Without disk encryption, stolen or decommissioned hardware exposes all stored data including credentials.
checks.remediation: Enable BitLocker on all fixed drives. Set EncryptionMethodWithXtsOs to 7 (XTS-AES-256) via GPO or Intune.
checks.rationale: BitLocker with XTS-AES-256 is the only protection against offline data theft from lost/stolen devices. Without disk encryption, an attacker with physical access can boot from a USB drive and read all data including the SAM database.

checks.impact: Without a startup PIN, stolen devices can be booted and attacked with DMA/cold boot.
checks.remediation: Enable "Require additional authentication at startup" with PIN requirement via GPO
checks.description: Require additional PIN authentication at BitLocker startup for enhanced protection
checks.name: Require BitLocker Startup PIN
checks.rationale: A BitLocker startup PIN adds a second factor to disk encryption — even if the TPM is compromised or the laptop motherboard is replaced, the PIN prevents boot. Defense against cold boot and DMA attacks on unattended devices.

checks.impact: Lost recovery keys mean permanent data loss on encrypted volumes.
checks.remediation: Configure recovery key backup to Azure AD/Entra ID via Intune or GPO
checks.description: Ensure BitLocker recovery keys are backed up to Entra ID or AD DS
checks.name: Configure BitLocker Recovery Key Backup
checks.rationale: Data protection settings ensure that data at rest and in transit is encrypted and that sensitive information cannot be extracted from lost, stolen, or decommissioned devices.

checks.impact: Unencrypted data drives expose stored files on stolen hardware.
checks.remediation: Enable BitLocker for fixed data drives via GPO or Intune
checks.description: Extend BitLocker encryption to all fixed data drives, not just the OS volume
checks.name: Encrypt Fixed Data Drives with BitLocker
checks.rationale: Data protection settings ensure that data at rest and in transit is encrypted and that sensitive information cannot be extracted from lost, stolen, or decommissioned devices.

checks.impact: Sleep mode preserves memory contents accessible via cold boot or DMA attacks.
checks.remediation: Set ACSettingIndex for sleep timeout to 0 (Never) via Power Plan GPO
checks.description: Disable sleep mode when device is plugged in to prevent memory-based attacks
checks.name: Disable Sleep While Plugged In
checks.rationale: Sleep mode preserves memory contents including encryption keys, credentials, and session tokens. Cold boot attacks can extract these by freezing RAM and reading the data. Disabling sleep on plugged-in devices forces hibernate or shutdown.

checks.impact: Hiberfil.sys contains a full memory dump including credentials and encryption keys.
checks.remediation: Set HibernateEnabled to 0 via power settings GPO or powercfg /hibernate off
checks.description: Disable hibernate to prevent memory dumps from being written to disk
checks.name: Disable Hibernate to Protect Memory Contents
checks.rationale: Hibernate writes the entire memory contents to hiberfil.sys on disk. This file contains everything in RAM — passwords, encryption keys, decrypted documents. Disabling hibernate prevents this persistent on-disk exposure.

checks.impact: Clipboard sharing allows easy bulk data transfer out of secured RDP sessions.
checks.remediation: Set fDisableClip to 1 under Terminal Services
checks.description: Prevent clipboard redirection in Remote Desktop sessions to block data exfiltration
checks.name: Disable Clipboard Sharing in RDP
checks.rationale: RDP clipboard sharing allows copy/paste between the remote session and local machine. While convenient, it enables easy bulk data exfiltration from secured environments. Disable for high-security RDP jump servers.

checks.impact: USB drives are a primary vector for data exfiltration in insider threats.
checks.remediation: Set Deny_Write to 1 under RemovableStorageDevices policy
checks.description: Prevent writing data to USB drives and removable storage to stop exfiltration
checks.name: Block Write Access to Removable Media
checks.rationale: USB drives are a primary vector for both data exfiltration (insider threats) and malware delivery (USB drop attacks). Deny_Write prevents data from being written to removable media.

checks.impact: Without HVCI, unsigned kernel drivers can be loaded to bypass all security controls.
checks.remediation: Enable Memory Integrity via Windows Security > Device Security > Core Isolation, or via Intune device configuration profile. Start without UEFI lock. Test with plug-in cards and peripheral devices before enforcing UEFI lock. Do not enable 'Require UEFI Memory Attributes Table' without testing.
checks.description: Enable Hypervisor-protected Code Integrity for kernel-mode code signing enforcement
checks.name: Enable Memory Integrity (HVCI)
checks.rationale: Memory Integrity (HVCI) ensures only signed kernel code runs by using the hypervisor to verify code integrity. The 23H2 baseline enabled this by default on compatible hardware. Like Credential Guard and LSA protection, HVCI supports 'enabled without UEFI lock' for initial deployment. Kernel Mode Hardware Enforced Stack Protection (22H2+) adds an additional layer. The 'Require UEFI Memory Attributes Table' option should only be enabled if you have confirmed all devices have compatible UEFI firmware — without MAT support, HVCI can cause crashes, data loss, or incompatibility with certain plug-in cards. Kernel DMA Protection blocks external peripherals from unauthorized memory access unless their drivers support DMA-remapping.

checks.impact: Error reports can contain memory dumps with sensitive data.
checks.remediation: Set DontSendAdditionalData to 1 under Windows Error Reporting policy
checks.description: Restrict Windows Error Reporting data to prevent inadvertent information disclosure
checks.name: Disable Windows Error Reporting Data Collection
checks.rationale: Data protection settings ensure that data at rest and in transit is encrypted and that sensitive information cannot be extracted from lost, stolen, or decommissioned devices.

checks.impact: Set BitLocker OS drive encryption to XTS-AES-256
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'EncryptionMethodWithXtsOs' to 7 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\FVE. Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\FVE\EncryptionMethodWithXtsOs. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.name: Configure Encryption Method for OS Drive (XTS-AES-256)
checks.description: Set BitLocker OS drive encryption to XTS-AES-256
checks.rationale: XTS-AES-256 is the strongest BitLocker encryption method. XTS mode provides better protection than CBC mode against manipulation of encrypted data. The baseline requires XTS-AES-256 (value 7) for OS drives.

checks.impact: Set BitLocker fixed drive encryption to XTS-AES-256

checks.name: Enforce Admin Approval Mode for All Administrators
checks.description: Enable Admin Approval Mode to ensure all admin accounts receive elevation prompts
checks.impact: Without Admin Approval Mode, admin accounts operate with full privileges silently, bypassing all UAC protections.
checks.remediation: Set EnableLUA to 1 (Enabled) via GPO or Intune CSP
checks.rationale: Admin Approval Mode (EnableLUA=1) is the foundation of UAC. Without it, all admin accounts run with full privileges silently — no elevation prompts, no integrity levels, no defense against accidental/malicious elevation.

checks.name: Configure Secure Desktop for Elevation Prompts
checks.description: Require elevation prompts to appear on the secure desktop to prevent UI spoofing
checks.impact: Without secure desktop, malware can overlay a fake elevation prompt to capture admin credentials.
checks.remediation: Set PromptOnSecureDesktop to 1 via GPO (Security Options) or Intune CSP
checks.rationale: The Secure Desktop is a separate desktop that only Windows kernel can access. Without it, malware can overlay a fake elevation prompt to capture admin credentials. PromptOnSecureDesktop=1 ensures the prompt is genuine.

checks.name: Set Elevation Prompt for Admins to Consent on Secure Desktop
checks.description: Require admin consent on the secure desktop when elevating, per the 24H2 baseline
checks.impact: Consent-only prompts are easier for users but provide less protection than credential prompts.
checks.remediation: Set ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin to 2 (Prompt for consent on secure desktop) via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: The 24H2 MDM baseline sets ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin=2 (Prompt for consent on the secure desktop). The new Administrator Protection feature (TypeOfAdminApprovalMode=2) replaces the traditional consent prompt with a Windows Security authentication prompt, creating a temporary admin token. ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin=1 (require credentials) is stricter but exceeds the baseline.

checks.name: Auto-Deny Elevation for Standard Users
checks.description: Automatically deny elevation requests from standard users without prompting for admin credentials
checks.impact: Prompting standard users for admin credentials creates a social engineering vector (users may ask admins to type passwords).
checks.remediation: Set ConsentPromptBehaviorUser to 0 (Auto deny) via GPO or Intune
checks.rationale: Auto-deny for standard users (ConsentPromptBehaviorUser=0) prevents the 'enter admin credentials' prompt that creates a social engineering vector. Users cannot elevate — they must contact IT.

checks.impact: Virtualization redirects legacy app writes to user-specific locations.
checks.remediation: Set EnableVirtualization to 1 under Policies\System
checks.description: Enable file and registry write virtualization for legacy application compatibility
checks.name: Enable File and Registry Write Virtualization
checks.rationale: File and registry write virtualization redirects failed writes (from legacy apps without manifest) to user-specific locations. EnableVirtualization=1 provides compatibility without compromising system integrity.

checks.impact: Without install detection, some legacy installers run without elevation prompts.
checks.remediation: Set EnableInstallerDetection to 1 under Policies\System
checks.description: Enable application install detection to prompt for elevation when needed
checks.name: Detect Application Installations and Prompt for Elevation
checks.rationale: UAC is the primary defense against unauthorized privilege escalation on Windows. Proper UAC configuration ensures that administrative actions require explicit authentication and prevents silent elevation.

checks.impact: Unsigned UIAccess applications can manipulate the secure desktop.
checks.remediation: Set ValidateAdminCodeSignatures to 1 under Policies\System
checks.description: Require code-signed executables for UIAccess privilege elevation
checks.name: Elevate Only Signed and Validated Executables
checks.rationale: ValidateAdminCodeSignatures=1 requires that only code-signed executables can request UIAccess privileges. Without this, unsigned applications could manipulate the UI to escalate privileges.

checks.impact: UIAccess from non-secure locations could allow malicious UI manipulation.
checks.remediation: Set EnableSecureUIAPaths to 1 under Policies\System
checks.description: Only elevate UIAccess applications installed in secure filesystem locations
checks.name: UIAccess Applications Require Secure Locations
checks.rationale: UAC is the primary defense against unauthorized privilege escalation on Windows. Proper UAC configuration ensures that administrative actions require explicit authentication and prevents silent elevation.

checks.impact: Without token filtering, admin accounts get full privileges on remote computers.
checks.remediation: Set LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy to 0 under Policies\System
checks.description: Enable filtering of administrator tokens on network logons to prevent remote elevation
checks.name: Filter Administrator Token on Network Logons
checks.rationale: LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy=0 ensures that admin tokens are filtered on network logons, preventing remote elevation with local admin accounts. When set to 1, pass-the-hash attacks work against local admin accounts remotely.

checks.impact: Run the built-in Administrator account in Admin Approval Mode
checks.remediation: GPO: Set 'FilterAdministratorToken' to 1 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System. Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\FilterAdministratorToken. Intune: Use Settings Catalog or custom OMA-URI if CSP mapping available.
checks.name: Configure Admin Approval Mode for Built-in Admin
checks.description: Run the built-in Administrator account in Admin Approval Mode
checks.rationale: UAC is the primary defense against unauthorized privilege escalation on Windows. Proper UAC configuration ensures that administrative actions require explicit authentication and prevents silent elevation.

checks.name: Install Missing Critical Security Updates
checks.description: Ensure all critical security updates are installed within the patching policy window
checks.impact: Unpatched systems are vulnerable to known exploits. Most ransomware targets available but unapplied patches.
checks.remediation: Run Windows Update or trigger deployment via WSUS/Intune. Review windowsUpdate.daysUnpatched.
checks.rationale: Most ransomware exploits known, patched vulnerabilities — the median time from patch release to exploit is 15 days (Mandiant M-Trends 2024). Systems unpatched beyond 30 days are at significantly elevated risk. The WannaCry attack in 2017 exploited a vulnerability patched 59 days earlier.

checks.name: Replace Unsigned Kernel-Mode Drivers
checks.description: Identify and replace unsigned drivers that bypass code integrity and may indicate malware
checks.impact: Unsigned kernel drivers bypass code integrity and can be exploited for privilege escalation.
checks.remediation: Update to signed versions, remove unused hardware drivers, or file vendor support cases.
checks.rationale: Unsigned kernel drivers bypass code integrity and can be exploited for privilege escalation (BYOVD — Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver). In 2022-2023, ransomware groups routinely used signed but vulnerable drivers to disable security tools.

checks.name: Investigate Unexpected System Shutdowns
checks.description: Review Kernel-Power Event 41 and Event 6008 indicating BSODs or unexpected power loss
checks.impact: Unexpected shutdowns cause data loss and indicate potential hardware failure or driver issues.
checks.remediation: For Event 41: check BugcheckCode. For Event 6008: verify power supply. Run memory/disk diagnostics.
checks.rationale: Kernel-Power Event 41 (unexpected shutdown) and Event 6008 (dirty shutdown) indicate BSODs or power loss. Recurring events suggest hardware failure, driver instability, or firmware issues that will eventually cause data loss.

checks.name: Resolve Frequent Application Crashes
checks.description: Investigate applications with more than 10 crash events (1000/1001) in the lookback period
checks.impact: Frequent crashes indicate compatibility issues, memory corruption, or missing dependencies.
checks.remediation: Group crashes by application name. Update, reinstall, or replace the crashing application.
checks.rationale: Application crash events (1000/1001) with >10 occurrences in 30 days indicate compatibility issues. Frequent crashes of security-critical processes (MsSense.exe, WinDefend) may indicate tamper attempts.

checks.impact: Broken Windows Update agent means no security patches are being applied.
checks.remediation: Check wuauserv service status. Run SFC /scannow and reset WU components if needed.
checks.description: Ensure the Windows Update service is running and configured correctly
checks.name: Verify Windows Update Agent Health
checks.rationale: Operational health checks identify issues that affect system stability, update currency, and maintenance status. These are proactive checks that prevent security gaps from developing over time.

checks.impact: End-of-life Windows versions receive no security updates.
checks.remediation: Check your current Windows version: Settings > System > About. Current supported versions: 25H2 (Build 26200), 24H2 (Build 26100), 23H2 (Build 22631), 22H2 (Build 22621). Plan feature update deployment via Intune Windows Update for Business ring or WSUS. End-of-life versions receive no security updates.
checks.description: Ensure the OS is running a supported Windows feature release version
checks.name: Update to Current Windows Feature Release
checks.rationale: End-of-life Windows versions receive no security updates, leaving the system permanently vulnerable to all newly discovered CVEs. Microsoft publishes end-of-service dates for each feature release on the Windows lifecycle fact sheet.

checks.name: Reduce Excessive Boot Time
checks.description: Investigate boot times exceeding 60 seconds indicating driver, service, or GPO startup issues
checks.impact: Slow boot times waste user productivity and often indicate deeper issues.
checks.remediation: Review boot performance events (Event 100/101). Defer non-critical services, remove unnecessary startup scripts.
checks.rationale: Boot times >60 seconds indicate driver, service, or GPO startup issues. Analyze Boot Performance events (Event 100/101) to identify slow-loading drivers and defer non-critical services. Long boot times also delay security agent initialization.

checks.impact: Slow logon times waste user productivity and indicate policy processing issues.
checks.remediation: Review User Environment event (1501/1502). Defer non-critical logon scripts.
checks.description: Investigate logon times exceeding 30 seconds indicating GPO or profile issues
checks.name: Reduce Excessive Logon Time
checks.rationale: Logon times >30 seconds indicate GPO processing issues, profile loading problems, or excessive logon scripts. Event 1501/1502 (User Environment) provides per-component timing. Review group policy processing with gpresult /h.

checks.impact: Extended uptime delays patches and driver updates that require reboot.
checks.remediation: Schedule a maintenance restart. Systems with >30 days uptime may have pending patches or driver updates that require a reboot. Check for pending reboot indicators in the registry (HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\WindowsUpdate\Auto Update\RebootRequired).
checks.description: Systems with uptime over 30 days may have missed restart-requiring patches
checks.name: Check System Uptime
checks.rationale: Systems with >30 days uptime have likely missed restart-requiring patches, driver updates, and kernel updates. The WinSxS store may also need maintenance. Schedule monthly maintenance restarts aligned with Patch Tuesday.

checks.description: Flag security event logs approaching capacity which may cause event loss
checks.name: Monitor Security Event Log Capacity
checks.impact: A full security log with circular overflow silently drops oldest events, creating audit blind spots.
checks.remediation: GPO: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Event Log Service > Security > Set 'Maximum Log Size' to at least 256 MB. Intune: Settings Catalog > Event Log. PowerShell: Limit-EventLog -LogName 'Security' -MaximumSize 262144KB. Also review audit policy to reduce noisy subcategories.
checks.rationale: When the security event log reaches capacity, oldest events are overwritten. If the log fills faster than review cycles, critical evidence (logon anomalies, privilege escalation, policy changes) can be lost before investigation. Monitoring usage percentage ensures proactive capacity management.

checks.description: Check for application hang events (1002) indicating UI responsiveness issues
checks.name: Monitor Application Hang Events
checks.impact: Frequent hangs degrade user productivity and may indicate driver or resource issues.
checks.remediation: Review Application event log for Event ID 1002. Identify hanging processes and check for driver conflicts, resource contention, or incompatible software. Consider updating or replacing problematic applications.
checks.rationale: Application hang events (Event 1002) indicate UI responsiveness issues. Recurring hangs for the same process suggest a systemic problem — driver conflict, resource exhaustion, or application bug — that should be investigated before it impacts multiple users.
